title: Paper clay abstract wall relief
subtitle: A layered paper clay wall panel with a sculptural surface
excerpt: An abstract wall panel built from a paper clay slab base with rolled and coiled clay elements pressed onto the surface to create a low-relief composition. Paper clay is strong enough to support thin appliqued details without cracking. The finished panel is sealed and hung. No kiln required.
sourceNotes: Paper clay wall relief technique derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: Liquid clay mixed with water to a cream consistency, used as an adhesive when joining clay pieces together.
The drying stage where clay holds its shape but is still damp enough to attach elements.
Scratching a surface with a needle tool and applying slip before joining two clay pieces to ensure a strong bond.

content.text: Paper clay holds large flat slabs together through drying without the warping or cracking that plain air-dry clay develops in thin sheets. This makes it the right material for a wall panel: the slab stays flat, and the appliqued detail survives drying without pulling away from the base.

Roll out a slab 6-8 mm thick and cut to the panel size: a 200 x 150 mm rectangle is a good starting size. Move the slab to the wooden board on which it will dry; paper clay can be moved at leather-hard but it is safest not to move large flat pieces. While the slab is fresh, press two small holes near the top edge, 15 mm in from each corner, for hanging.
Allow the slab to stiffen to
before adding relief elements. This usually takes 1-2 hours under normal conditions.
is essential for every element: scratch the back of the piece and the panel surface, apply
to both, then press the element on firmly.

Abstract elements to try: rolled coils pressed into curved lines, small flattened spheres, thin rolled slabs torn into irregular shapes and layered, and short cylinders of different diameters pressed flat-end-down. Vary the heights to create depth across the composition. Leave some of the base slab visible to frame the relief.

Dry slowly for 2-3 days. If the board is smooth, the slab should release naturally as it dries and contracts slightly. If it sticks, slide a palette knife underneath. Once bone-dry, sand any rough edges with fine sandpaper. Seal the whole panel with diluted PVA (50:50 with water) or a spray acrylic sealant. Two coats give a durable surface. Hang via the pre-cut holes using two wall screws or a piece of cord through both holes.
Paint the base slab with acrylic wash before adding elements, so the base colour contrasts with the relief.
Press leaf or fern fronds into the still-soft base slab before the relief layer to add background texture.
Make a set of three small panels (100 x 80 mm each) with related motifs and hang as a triptych.

rows.problem: Relief elements fall off during drying
rows.cause: Score and slip was not applied, or elements were added to a dry rather than leather-hard base
rows.fix: Re-attach fallen pieces with fresh slip on both surfaces. If the base has dried fully, roughen both surfaces with sandpaper and use PVA as an adhesive after drying.

rows.problem: Base slab warps upward at the corners
rows.cause: Slab was too thin or dried too fast
rows.fix: Weight the corners with small stones during the first 12 hours of drying, or dry the panel sandwiched loosely between two boards.

rows.problem: Cracks develop in the base during drying
rows.cause: Paper clay was over-watered during rolling, or the slab was moved at the wrong drying stage
rows.fix: Fill small cracks with fresh paper clay slip once the panel is bone-dry, then reseal.
